summary: I was born and raised in Sweden, in a small town of about 3,000 people. My family was middle-class, with my father working in plant breeding and my mother as an elementary school teacher. Growing up in Sweden, I experienced the social and political changes firsthand, like the introduction of universal healthcare when I was around 18 or 19. I also remember the strong presence of the Lutheran Church in Swedish life before 1959, even though my family was quite secular.

My father was a member of Högerpartiet, the conservative party in Sweden, which he proudly supported during my childhood. I even attended youth dances organized by Högerpartiet. Interestingly, my father once joked that Högerpartiet was as conservative as George McGovern, highlighting how Swedish conservatives were not very conservative compared to American standards. Despite his affiliation, towards the end of his life, he voted for the Social Democrats.
cues: father's later vote for Social Democrats

summary: I recall my father humorously saying that Högerpartiet, the Swedish conservative party, was as conservative as George McGovern, which was a funny comparison given McGovern's liberal stance in the U.S. This comment reflected the difference in political spectrums between Sweden and America during those times. It was a lighthearted way to explain the moderate nature of Swedish conservatives to me.

My father was very impressed by Tage Erlander, who was the Prime Minister of Sweden during my childhood. Although my father belonged to the conservative Högerpartiet, he respected Erlander's leadership and the Social Democrats' policies. This admiration influenced some of the political discussions in our household, especially as my father eventually voted for the Social Democrats later in life.

The Social Democrats played a significant role in my upbringing, especially through my mother who was progressive and loved what they had done for Sweden. I remember discussions about universal healthcare, a Social Democratic initiative, which was introduced when I was about 18 or 19. Even my conservative father ended up voting for the Social Democrats toward the end of his life, showing their broad appeal in Sweden.

Although my family was quite secular, the Lutheran Church was a big part of Swedish life when I was growing up. Before 1959, everyone was born into the Lutheran Church, which kept official records of births, deaths, and moves. In our small town, my father and my brother's best friend were among the very few who formally exited the church after the law changed in 1959. So, while we weren't religious, the church's presence was always felt in the background.

During the Finnish Winter War, Finland sent many children to Sweden for safety, and my family took in one boy named Esko. His father was also a plant breeder like my father, which made the arrangement somewhat personal. We have a document from the Finnish President thanking us for caring for Esko. This experience led to ongoing exchanges between our families, with my brother eventually visiting Finland and Esko becoming a doctor.

Finland was very much part of my life during the Finnish Winter War when many Finnish children were sent to Sweden for safety. We hosted a Finnish boy named Esko, whose father was a plant breeder like my own father. This connection fostered a long-lasting relationship, with my brother visiting Finland after the war and our families exchanging visits over the years. Finland's situation during the war brought a personal dimension to our family experience.

I moved to Seattle seeking adventure after growing up in Sweden. My father had visited the United States a few times and spoke highly of it, especially the University of Minnesota, which inspired me. After arriving, I stayed with a sponsor's family and eventually got a job at the Laboratory of Human Development at Harvard, but Seattle became my new home. It was a big change from my small Swedish town, but it opened many new opportunities for me.

Stockholm was the city that sent down the math test that my students took at the end of their schooling. When I was teaching in Sweden, the final exams were standardized and administered from Stockholm, which was the center of educational authority. Passing that test was a big achievement for my difficult class, and it was a proud moment when none of them failed.

After arriving in the United States, I got a job at the Laboratory of Human Development at Harvard University through a connection with a Danish girl. This was an exciting opportunity early in my American adventure. Working at Harvard gave me valuable experience and helped me establish myself in the U.S., even though my ultimate residence became Seattle.
cues: job at Laboratory of Human Development

summary: My father had been to the University of Minnesota in Minneapolis for his research in plant breeding, and he always spoke very positively about the United States because of that. His experiences there inspired me to come to America and seek my own adventure. The University of Minnesota was part of the reason I was drawn to the U.S., even though I eventually worked elsewhere.

Richard Nixon was not directly mentioned in my story, but I recall political discussions during my time in the United States, which would have overlapped with his presidency. My father's comparisons between Swedish conservatives and American politicians like George McGovern gave me some perspective on U.S. politics, which included figures like Nixon. However, Nixon himself was not a central figure in my personal narrative.
